top_categories.workout_context: At mile 60 of 100, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
9,000ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
90ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 100 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Gran Fondo Utah's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before June.
At Gran Fondo Utah, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
Gran Fondo Utah's mixed terrain forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 100 miles.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Gran Fondo Utah. Train it until it feels boring.
9,000ft of climbing on grades that change every minute. You need to switch from seated to standing without losing rhythm.
100 miles rewards fat oxidation. This volume work shifts your fuel mix so you're still burning fat at hour 5, not bonking at hour 3.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 9,000ft of climbing on mixed terrain does to your power file on race day.
How long can you hold 105% FTP before you blow? At Gran Fondo Utah, that number decides whether you bridge or get dropped.

race_overlay.heat: June in Payson Memorial Park, 300 S Main St, Payson, UT 84651, USA can bring heat. Complete 3–4 heat exposure sessions in the final 2 weeks before Gran Fondo Utah. Increase sodium intake 48 hours before race day.
race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for Gran Fondo Utah’s 100 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.
race_overlay.altitude: Gran Fondo Utah with 9,000ft of climbing, much of it above 8,000ft demands altitude preparation. Arrive 5–7 days early for acclimatization. Expect 5–15% power reduction at altitude. Increase iron intake 4 weeks out. Hydrate aggressively — altitude increases fluid loss by 20–40%.
race_overlay.terrain: Mixed terrain at Gran Fondo Utah requires surface adaptability. Include weekly rides on mixed terrain to build confidence.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Durability, VO2max, and TT Threshold to prepare you for 100 miles of mixed terrain in Payson Memorial Park, 300 S Main St, Payson, UT 84651, USA.
